data.content:
Here are the three MSBuild evaluation performance issues in this project:

---

### 1. Deep Import Chain (`level1 → level2 → level3`)

`EvalHeavy.csproj` imports `level1.props`, which imports `level2.props`, which imports `level3.props`. Each `<Import>` is resolved during the **evaluation phase** — MSBuild must locate, parse, and merge every file before any target runs. Deep/nested import chains multiply this cost linearly and make evaluation order harder to reason about. Flatten these into a single `.props` or reduce nesting where possible.

### 2. Overly Broad Glob Pattern

```xml
<AdditionalFiles Include="**\*.*" Exclude="**\bin\**;**\obj\**" />
```

The `**\*.*` glob forces MSBuild's evaluator to **enumerate every file in the entire directory tree**. It only excludes `bin` and `obj`, so it will still crawl into `.git/`, `node_modules/`, `packages/`, or any other large directory that happens to exist. This is expensive on large repos.

**Fix:** Restrict the glob to specific extensions or directories, and leverage `DefaultItemExcludes`:

```xml
<AdditionalFiles Include="**\*.cs;**\*.json"
                 Exclude="$(DefaultItemExcludes);**\bin\**;**\obj\**" />
```

### 3. File I/O via Property Function During Evaluation

```xml
<BuildNotes Condition="Exists('build-notes.txt')">
  $([System.IO.File]::ReadAllText('build-notes.txt'))
</BuildNotes>
```

`[System.IO.File]::ReadAllText(...)` is a **property function** that performs synchronous disk I/O at evaluation time. MSBuild evaluation is supposed to be fast and side-effect-free — it determines property values and item lists before any targets execute. Reading file contents here blocks the evaluator. Move this to a target so it runs during the **execution phase** instead:

```xml
<Target Name="ReadBuildNotes" BeforeTargets="Build">
  <ReadLinesFromFile File="build-notes.txt" Condition="Exists('build-notes.txt')">
    <Output TaskParameter="Lines" PropertyName="BuildNotes" />
  </ReadLinesFromFile>
</Target>
```

---

**Key concept — evaluation vs. execution:** MSBuild processes a project in two phases. The *evaluation phase* parses all imports, expands properties, and resolves item globs. The *execution phase* runs targets and tasks. Expensive work (file I/O, broad globs, deep import trees) during evaluation slows down every `dotnet` invocation — even `dotnet restore` or IDE design-time builds — because evaluation always runs first.
